coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/carbon dots modified cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 gel bead: An effective and easily separable photocatalytic adsorbent for Cr(VI) removal

coredata.dc:description: To tackle the concern of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) contamination, a photocatalytic adsorbent, chitosan/carbon dots modified cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 gel bead (CCCZ), was synthesized as an effective and easily separable material for Cr(VI) removal. A comprehensive analysis of CCCZ encompassing diverse analytical techniques was conducted to explore its morphology, structure, optical properties, as well as its adsorption and photocatalytic behaviors. Adsorption experiments revealed the exceptional adsorption capacity of CCCZ for Cr(VI), with a maximum capacity of 500.08 mg/g, surpassing that of original chitosan gel bead by a factor of 3.4. Moreover, the results from the adsorption and photocatalytic experiments provided robust evidence of CCCZ's superior performance. CCCZ exhibited an apparent rate constant of 0.1150 min−1, which was significantly higher (5.7 times) than that of pure ZIF-8. Remarkably, CCCZ exhibited an efficient regeneration capacity alongside its substantiated long-term stability. This study yields invaluable insights into the development of novel, high-efficiency photocatalytic adsorbents featuring facile separability, directed toward the effective alleviation of Cr(VI) contaminant.

head.ce:title: Chitosan/carbon dots modified cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 gel bead: An effective and easily separable photocatalytic adsorbent for Cr(VI) removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To tackle the concern of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) contamination, a photocatalytic adsorbent, chitosan/carbon dots modified cellulose nanofibrils/ZIF-8 gel bead (CCCZ), was synthesized as an effective and easily separable material for Cr(VI) removal. A comprehensive analysis of CCCZ encompassing diverse analytical techniques was conducted to explore its morphology, structure, optical properties, as well as its adsorption and photocatalytic behaviors. Adsorption experiments revealed the exceptional adsorption capacity of CCCZ for Cr(VI), with a maximum capacity of 500.08 mg/g, surpassing that of original chitosan gel bead by a factor of 3.4. Moreover, the results from the adsorption and photocatalytic experiments provided robust evidence of CCCZ's superior performance. CCCZ exhibited an apparent rate constant of 0.1150 min−1, which was significantly higher (5.7 times) than that of pure ZIF-8. Remarkably, CCCZ exhibited an efficient regeneration capacity alongside its substantiated long-term stability. This study yields invaluable insights into the development of novel, high-efficiency photocatalytic adsorbents featuring facile separability, directed toward the effective alleviation of Cr(VI) contaminant.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive utilization of chromium across various industries, including leather tanning, electroplating, dye synthesis, metal finishing, and steel manufacturing, leads to the contamination of the environment with Cr(VI) [1]. Overconsumption of Cr(VI) can lead to severe health risks, including kidney damage, anemia, nervous system dysfunction, and malignancies affecting the digestive tract and lungs [2]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have established stringent maximum allowable thresholds for Cr(VI) concentration in potable water, set at 100 and 50 ppb, respectively [3]. Various approaches, such as ion exchange [4], chemical precipitation [5], membrane processes [6], adsorption [7], and photocatalysis [8], have been proposed for removing Cr(VI) from aqueous solutions. Among these methods, adsorption has gained widespread attention due to its efficiency, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness [9]. Additionally, photocatalysis holds promise in mitigating the toxicity of Cr(VI) by converting it into less harmful Cr(III) species [10]. The photocatalytic process is usually conducted under mild conditions, eliminating the need for elevated temperatures, excessive pressures, or harmful solvents. This characteristic helps reduce energy consumption and minimize chemical waste production [11]. The synergy between adsorption and photocatalysis could offer a strategy with significant potential. Thus, the development of photocatalytic adsorbents holds a promising avenue for significantly enhancing the effectiveness of Cr(VI) elimination.

Chitosan (CS), a polysaccharide derived from biological sources, has garnered significant attention as a highly promising polymer for the development of innovative adsorbents [12]. Lu et al. synthesized CS beads impregnated with nano iron oxide to remove Cr(VI), achieving an adsorption capacity of 39.8 mg/g at pH 5.0 and a temperature of 20 °C [13]. Zhang et al. created multicavity triethylenetetramine-CS/alginate composite beads with the aim of Cr(VI) removal and elucidated multiple underlying mechanisms [14]. Zhang et al. synthesized CS gel beads fortified with malic acid to target the efficient Cr(VI) removal [15]. CS-based gel beads exhibit significant potential for Cr(VI) removal. To enhance their adsorption performance, supplementary nanomaterials have been incorporated. Cellulose nanofibrils (CN) emerge as notable among these nanomaterials due to their ability to create a porous structure that facilitates the accumulation and adsorption of Cr(VI) [16]. Consequently, CN holds great potential as an enhancer for the performance of CS-based gel beads.

Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) exhibits considerable potential in diverse applications, notably in adsorption and photocatalysis [17]. Shahrak et al. fabricated ZIF-8 for Cr(VI) adsorption and subsequently verified its effective adsorption performance [18]. Qiu et al. successfully developed a highly efficient photocatalyst termed Cd0.5Zn0.5S@ZIF-8 nanocomposite, specifically designed for Cr(VI) reduction. Their investigation furnished compelling evidence affirming ZIF-8′s promising potential in the realm of photocatalytic material design [19]. Despite the demonstrated effectiveness of ZIF-8 in adsorption and photocatalysis, a significant practical challenge arises its powdered form. ZIF-8 in this form is difficult to prepare, exhibits poor distribution, and readily aggregates, thereby impeding its effective utilization [20]. To tackle this issue effectively, immobilizing ZIF-8 within a suitable matrix, such as a CS-based gel bead, presents a viable approach [21]. This immobilization not only holds the potential to augment the adsorption capacity of the gel bead but also introduces a novel photocatalytic function.

Carbon dots (CD) belong to the category of carbon-based nanomaterials and exhibit several advantageous characteristics, including remarkable fluorescent properties, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, and cost-effective production [22]. Moreover, numerous studies have documented the significance of CD in facilitating targeted interactions with other molecules [23]. Yuan et al. fabricated a lignin-based hydrogel incorporating CD. Subsequent adsorption experiments provided conclusive evidence for the positive effect of CD on Cr(VI) adsorption. Additionally, CD exhibit exceptional electronic properties, rendering them excellent electron donors and acceptors. Leveraging their superior electron transfer capabilities, CD have been integrated into photocatalytic systems to effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of other materials. Li et al. utilized a facile hydrothermal deposition method to prepare CD/ZnFe2O4 yolk-shell composites, capitalizing on the surface group interaction between CD and ZnFe2O4. The incorporation of CD led to a significant 25% enhancement in catalytic efficiency, supported by their findings [24]. However, similar to numerous other carbon-based nanomaterials, CD exhibit inherent weaknesses including poor stability and susceptibility to agglomeration. However, suitable support can solve this drawback [25]. Of significance, this integration of CD into the original CS-based gel beads offers an opportunity to enhance the adsorption capacity and improve the photocatalytic capabilities.

The objective of this study was to formulate a novel photocatalytic adsorbent, CCCZ, comprising CS, CD, CN, and ZIF-8, intended for long-term stability and easy separation, for efficient removal of Cr(VI). Comprehensive characterization was performed on the synthesized CCCZ using various analytical techniques, with a focus on its adsorption and photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the investigation delved into the mechanism underpinning the successful Cr(VI) removal through CCCZ.
The reagents were described in the Supplementary material.

Bleached wood pulp was employed for the production of CN [26]. First, 0.05 g of 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO), 0.3 g of sodium bromide and 250 g of water were introduced to 2.5 g of bleached wood pulp. Subsequently, 20 g of sodium hypochlorite solution (10 wt%) was added to the suspension. A pH of 10 was attained by adding 0.1 M sodium hydroxide. After five hours, the resulting TEMPO-oxidized cellulose fibers were washed seven times with water until the pH reached 7. Subsequently, the mixture underwent six cycles of homogenization using a M110P microfluidizer. Ultimately, the CN were collected and concentrated to a 1.0 wt% by judicious addition of water.

A total of 2.1 g of citric acid and 6.37 mL of ethylenediamine were introduced into 50 mL of water. The solution was placed in an autoclave reactor. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to hydrothermal heating for five hours at 200 °C. The resulting CD solution underwent a 5-day dialysis process to achieve further purification.

The covalent attachment of CD onto CN was achieved employing 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) [27]. A solution containing 0.10 g of EDC and 10.0 g of water were added to a 10.0 g suspension of CN (0.5 wt%) and subjected to stirring for 15 min. Following this, 0.12 g of NHS was added and the mixture was subjected to an additional 15 min of stirring. Subsequently, a 1.3 g CD solution (0.5 wt%) was added to the reaction flask and the pH was adjusted to 5 by adding 0.1 M HCl. The reaction was allowed to proceed overnight with continuous stirring. The resulting sample, denoted CN-CD, underwent a 5-day dialysis process to eliminate any residual impurities. The energy level positions of CN-CD were discerned through utilization of UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) and valence band X-ray photoelectron (VB-XPS) spectra (Fig. S1).

The acetic acid solution of CS was prepared by mixing 6 g of CS with 144 g of a 2.0 wt% acetic acid solution under continuous stirring until a homogeneous solution was attained. Likewise, the acetic acid solution of CN-CD was prepared by dissolving a 1.0 wt% CN-CD suspension (50 g) in a 2.0 wt% acetic acid solution (100 g) through continuous stirring to achieve homogeneity. Solution A was prepared by dissolving 0.5 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate in 10 g of the prepared acetic acid solution of CS combined with 3 g of the prepared acetic acid solution of CN-CD. Solution B was produced by dissolving 5.85 g of 2-Methylimidazole in 70 mL of a 2 mol/L NaOH solution. Subsequently, Solution A was introduced into Solution B via a syringe pump (LSP01-1BH, Longer) equipped with a stainless steel needle (inner diameter: 0.26 mm) set at a flow rate of 10 mL/min. The resulting microspheres were meticulously washed with water until a neutral pH was achieved. Thereafter, they were mixed with 40 mL of a 3.6 wt% glutaraldehyde solution and heated at 45 °C for 12 h to yield the ultimate product. The resulting products were designated as CCCZ-1, CCCZ-2, CCCZ-3, and CCCZ-4, denoting varying quantities of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (0.6, 0.5, 0.3, and 0.1 g) and 2-Methylimidazole (7.02, 5.85, 3.51, and 1.17 g), correspondingly. Furthermore, control gel beads labeled as CCZ-0 and CCZ-2 were fabricated by substituting CN–CD with CN. The mass of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was 0 g for CCZ-0 and 0.3 g for CCZ-2, whereas the mass of 2-Methylimidazole was 0 g for CCZ-0 and 3.51 g for CCZ-2. SEM, XPS and FTIR analysis of pure ZIF-8, CCZ-0, and CCZ-2 was presented in Fig. S2.

During the adsorption experiment, the dose of gel bead was 0.04 g (dry weight), the volume of Cr(VI) solution was 200 mL, and the stirring rate was set to 300 rpm. If no otherwise specified, the initial Cr(VI) concentration (C0) remained at 20 mg/L, the contact time (t) was maintained for 24 h, the pH was adjusted to 2 and the temperature (T) was held at 25 °C. The effect of pH on adsorption was tested at pH 2–5. The effect of T on adsorption was assessed over the range of 15–55 °C. Adsorption isotherms were established through batch experiments using diverse Cr(VI) concentrations (10–50 mg/L). Additionally, adsorption kinetics experiments were examined through experiments involving various contact times (8–40 min). Each experiment was repeated thrice to account for potential errors. Cr(VI) concentration was determined using the 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method with a UV–visible spectrophotometer at 540 nm.

The experiment aimed to evaluate the removal efficiency and the photocatalytic reaction rate constant [28]. To achieve this, a specific amount of the sample was introduced into a reaction solution containing Cr(VI) ions (200 mL) and the mixture was then kept in darkness for 40 min. Afterwards, the suspension was exposed to a 500 W high-pressure mercury lamp for 40 min. At 8-minute intervals, a 5 mL aliquot was filtered through syringe filters, and the concentration of the filtered solution was determined. The removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was calculated using the equation: “
Removal efficiency(\%)
=
(
C 0
- C
t
)
/
C 0
”. In this study, the total chromium (Cr) concentrations were assessed via an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (TAS-990, Beijing General Instrument, China). The concentration of Cr(III) was subsequently calculated based on the difference between the total Cr concentration and that of Cr(VI). Simultaneously, the apparent rate constant (kapp ) was determined using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model, as detailed in the Supplementary material. Additionally, photographs illustrating these experiments were presented in Fig. S3. To compare the impact of different light sources, the adsorption and photocatalytic activities of CCCZ-2 were explored under both UV irradiation and LED light (Fig. S4).

The surface topography was investigated using SEM (ZEISS GeminiSEM 300, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of the prepared sample was performed with a Thermo Fisher IN 10TM spectrometer from USA. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were acquired with a Rigaku D/MAX2500 XRD instrument from the United Kingdom. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–vis DRS) of the prepared sample was acquired within the 400–800 nm range using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer from Japan. Using an XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA), the binding energies of samples before and after adsorption were discovered. The mechanical properties of the fabricated gel beads were evaluated with a Texture Analyzer (CT3 10 K, Brookfield Engineering Laboratories, USA). Comprehensive electrochemical measurement experiments, including photocurrent density data and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were provided in the Supplementary material.

Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic fabrication process of CCCZ, composed of CS, CN, CD, and ZIF-8 as the primary constituents. Initially, CD were synthesized via a hydrothermal reaction [29]. Concurrently, CN was prepared through TEMPO-mediated oxidation and high-pressure homogenization, leading to CN enriched with a notable quantity of carboxyl groups [30]. Subsequently, CD was attached to CN, leading to the formation of CN-CD [31]. Following that, a solution A was prepared by dissolving zinc nitrate hexahydrate in a CS solution comprising both of CS and CN-CD. Concurrently, NaOH and 2-Methylimidazole (Hmim) were dissolved in deionized water, resulting in the creation of solution B. Subsequently, solution A was mixed with solution B using a stainless steel needle, enabling the production of chitosan/CN-CD/ZIF-8 microspheres. Lastly, the CCCZ was synthesized with the aid of glutaraldehyde.

SEM was employed to analyze the morphology of the prepared samples. In Fig. 2 a, CN displayed a smooth and fibrous structure. CD, depicted in Fig. S5, exhibited a spherical shape. The SEM image of CN-CD (Fig. 2b) indicated a rough surface, suggesting the successful loading of CD onto CN [32]. Moreover, SEM images of CCCZ-2 (Fig. 2c and 2d) revealed a distinct three-dimensional (3D) porous structure.

To characterize the synthesized samples, XRD analysis was conducted, and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 2e. CS exhibited a single broad peak at 20.1°, signifying its amorphous structure [33]. XRD patterns of CN and CN-CD had three distinct bands at 12.1°, 16.0°, and 22.5°, corresponding to the (11′0), (110), and (200) crystal planes of cellulose, respectively [34]. Conversely, CCCZ-2 exhibited seven peaks at 10.4°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, 18.0°, 24.5°, and 26.7°, assigned to the (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (233), and (134) crystalline planes of ZIF-8 [35]. These findings provided strong evidence for the in-situ generation of ZIF-8 in CCCZ-2. However, the peak associated with CS was absent due to the crosslinking reaction that CS underwent [36]. Furthermore, the two main peaks associated with CN-CD disappeared upon incorporation into CCCZ-2, indicating the disruption of their crystalline structures.

Fig. 2f depicts the FTIR spectra of the prepared samples. In the CS spectrum, the bending vibration of − NH2 was attributed to the peak at 1550 cm−1 [37]. In the spectra of CN and CN-CD, a prominent C–O stretching vibration band was observed at 1053 cm−1 [38]. In CN-CD, a new band at 1650 cm−1 belonging to stretching vibration of –CO–NH– appeared, suggesting the formation of a chemical bond between CD and CN. CCCZ-2 displayed two distinct peaks at 756 cm−1 and 693 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of Zn–O and Zn–N, respectively [39], confirming the presence of ZIF-8. Furthermore, a characteristic peak at 1647 cm−1 was observed, corresponding to the stretching vibration of –CC–NC–, signifying the formation of a cross-linked structure between CS and glutaraldehyde [40].

The composition analysis of CCCZ-2 was performed utilizing XPS, as shown in Fig. 2g. The XPS survey spectrum indicated the presence of Zn, O, N, and C elements in CCCZ-2. High-resolution spectra of Zn2p were obtained and presented in Fig. 2h.The Zn2p spectrum exhibited two distinct peaks at 1021.4 eV and 1044.4 eV, corresponding to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, respectively [41]. These findings offered compelling evidence supporting the in-situ generation of ZIF-8 in CCCZ-2 [42].

N2 adsorption–desorption testing was performed to characterize the porosity and specific surface area of the fabricated gel beads. Fig. S6 displays the N2 adsorption–desorption curves for CCZ-0, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2, along with their respective specific surface areas (S BET) and average pore diameters (d p), as listed in Table S1. Notably, CCZ-2 exhibited a significantly higher S BET (823.4 m2/g) than CCZ-0 (155.4 m2/g), attributed to the in-situ generation of ZIF-8. Importantly, CCCZ-2 exhibited superior S BET and d p compared to CCZ-2, attributed to the inclusion of CD. These findings highlight the substantial specific surface area and pore size of CCCZ-2, indicating its potential for efficient Cr(VI) adsorption.

Mechanical properties of the fabricated gel beads were evaluated utilizing a texture analyzer. The test outcomes for the gel beads, subjected to two testing cycles, are presented in Fig. S7. Following two testing cycles, a minor decrease in hardness was noted for CCCZ-2, with measured values of 492.2 g for hardness and 0.98 for elasticity. A comprehensive breakdown of hardness and elasticity values for the fabricated beads is presented in Table S2. Notably, CCCZ-2 exhibited the highest levels of hardness and elasticity. These observations demonstrated that the enhancement of hardness and elasticity was accomplished through the incorporation of ZIF-8 and CD.

The potential toxicity of CCCZ-2 was assessed by incubating it with NIH/3T3 cells using the CCK-8 assay [43]. Fig. S8 illustrates cell viability after 24-hour incubation with varying CCCZ-2 concentrations (0–200 μg/mL), consistently showing cell viability above 90%. Furthermore, the results of the live/dead staining assay conducted after 24 h on CCCZ-2 are presented in Fig. S9. The abundance of blue dots representing live cells far surpassed the limited presence of red dots indicating dead cells. The absence of toxicity in CCCZ-2 can be attributed to the in-situ hydrothermal reaction, effectively reducing chemical component loss and enhancing stability. Consequently, QNIW-2 was suitable for water treatment.
The equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) of CCCZ-2 was significantly influenced by three key parameters: pH, initial concentration of Cr(VI) (C0), and temperature (T), as illustrated in Fig. 3 a-c.

The adsorption behavior of CCCZ-2 was examined with respect to pH, as illustrated in Fig. 3d. The results demonstrated a decrease in the Qe. Within the acidic pH range of 1–6.9, Cr(VI) predominantly existed in negatively charged forms. Importantly, the point of zero charge (pHPZC) of CCCZ-2 was determined to be approximately 5.1 (Fig. S10), indicating its protonation ability below pH 5.1 [44]. At pH 2, the maximum Qe was observed. Nevertheless, within the pH range of 2–5, the decrease in Qe resulted from a reduced protonation level of CCCZ-2, negatively impacting electrostatic adsorption. Consequently, pH 2 was established as the optimal condition.

The adsorption behavior of CCCZ-2 was investigated with respect to T, as shown in Fig. 3e. The results indicated an increased Qe, suggesting a spontaneous endothermic nature of the adsorption process [45]. For practical reasons, a T of 25 °C was chosen.

Fig. 3f shows the Qe of CCZ-0, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 as a function of C0. The increase in C0 from 10 to 50 mg/L resulted in higher Qe for both materials. Notably, CCCZ-2 exhibited the highest Qe at the same C0, highlighting the beneficial impact of CD and ZIF-8 on its adsorption capacity [46]. To elucidate the adsorption mechanism of CCCZ-2, the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were employed and their corresponding plots are provided in Fig. 3h and 3i, respectively [47]. The parameters for fitting these models are presented in Table S3. The Langmuir model exhibited a higher correlation coefficient (R2) than the Freundlich model, suggesting a better fit for the adsorption isotherm data of CCCZ-2. Moreover, the Langmuir model was employed to ascertain the maximum adsorption capacity [48], calculated as 500.08 mg/g, which exceeded the CS gel bead's adsorption capacity by 3.4-fold (Fig. S11). Remarkably, CCCZ-2 demonstrated remarkable efficiency as a Cr(VI) ion adsorbent, with notably higher adsorption capacity compared to other adsorbents (Table 1 ).

Fig. 3g illustrates the impact of contact time on adsorption capacity [59]. The adsorption capacity (Qt) was observed to increase over time. The observed increase in Qt for CCCZ-2 can be attributed to the favorable impact of CD and ZIF-8 on Cr(VI) adsorption [60]. To elucidate the adsorption mechanism, two kinetic models, namely the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models, were employed to analyze the adsorption process of CCCZ-2 [61]. The respective fitting parameters are detailed in Table S4, and visual representation of the outcomes is provided in Fig. 3j and 3 k. The goodness-of-fit analysis confirmed the superiority of the pseudo-second-order model, suggesting that the adsorption behavior of CCCZ-2 could be attributed to chemical adsorption.

The adsorption and photocatalytic activities of three synthesized samples, namely ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2, were investigated [62]. To establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the mixture was stirred in darkness for 40 min prior to UV irradiation. Removal efficiencies of ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 were evaluated over time and are depicted in Fig. 4 a. The results showed that after 40 min of UV light exposure, ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 exhibited Cr(VI) removal percentages of 55.6%, 79.0%, and 98.8%, respectively. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood model was used to determine the kapp , as illustrated in Fig. 4b. The obtained results demonstrated that CCCZ-2 exhibited a significantly higher k (0.1150 min−1) compared to ZIF-8 (0.0202 min−1) and CCZ-2 (0.0375 min−1), exceeding them by 5.7 and 3.1 times, respectively. Additionally, variations in the concentrations of total Cr, Cr(VI), and Cr(III) during the adsorption and photocatalysis process with ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 were shown in Fig. S12. Both the reduction in Cr(VI) concentration and the increase in Cr(III) concentration followed the order: ZIF-8 < CCZ-2 < CCCZ-2. These results confirmed that CCCZ-2 exhibited the most superior photocatalytic ability [63], largely attributed to the CN-CD loading. The inclusion of CN-CD loading could enhance the specific surface area, facilitate the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and thereby improve the removal efficiency of CCCZ-2.

This study examined the impact of pH on Cr(VI) removal by CCCZ-2, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. Notably, CCCZ-2 demonstrated removal efficiencies of 98.8%, 58.2%, 49.5%, and 34.1% at pH 2, 3, 4, and 5, respectively, over a 40-minute exposure period. These results indicated that acidic conditions favored Cr(VI) removal. The corresponding kapp values for CCCZ-2 were determined as 0.1150, 0.0202, 0.0151, and 0.0093 min−1, as depicted in Fig. 4d. The study highlighted the significant impact of pH on Cr(VI) removal [64], leading to the determination of an optimal pH of 2 for CCCZ-2.

The effect of varying ZIF-8 quantities in CCCZ-x was evaluated by synthesizing samples with x values of 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively. The results, presented in Fig. 4e, showed an initial increase in removal efficiency with an increase in ZIF-8 loading, followed by a subsequent decrease [65]. Furthermore, Fig. 4f depicted the kapp of CCCZ-x. Remarkably, CCCZ-2 exhibited the highest kapp value (0.1150 min−1), exceeding the kapp values of CCCZ-1, CCCZ-3, and CCCZ-4 by factors of 5.2, 1.8, and 10.5, respectively. These findings suggested that CCCZ-2 corresponded to the optimal ZIF-8 loading amount.

This study investigated the impact of C0 on the removal of Cr(VI) by CCCZ-2. The C0 range varied from 10 to 40 mg/L, as shown in Fig. 4g. The results indicated a negative correlation between the C0 and removal efficiency. For the C0 below 20 mg/L, removal efficiency approached 100% within a 40-minute UV light exposure. The kapp of CCCZ-2 was determined for four C0 values: 0.1350, 0.1150, 0.0495, and 0.0321 min−1, as shown in Fig. 4h. Furthermore, the study observed that competitive adsorption intensified at higher initial concentrations [66]. Based on these findings, it could be concluded that an optimal C0 was below 20 mg/L was.

The potential application of CCCZ-2 was assessed through tests of its reusability and stability. CCCZ-2 exhibited remarkable reusability, retaining more than 89.7% of its initial removal efficiency after five cycles (Fig. 5 a). Unlike ZIF-8, CCZ-0, and CCZ-2 (Fig. S13), CCCZ-2 demonstrated the highest reusability. Additionally, CCCZ-2 showcased significant temporal stability, preserving over 92.7% of its initial removal efficiency even after twelve weeks of storage, as shown in Fig. 5b. FTIR and XRD spectra of CCCZ-2 were analyzed after five reuse cycles, as shown in Fig. 5c and 5e. Additionally, FTIR and XRD spectra of CCCZ-2 were obtained after a twelve-week storage period, as depicted in Fig. 5d and f. Importantly, the distinctive peak positions in both the reused and stored CCCZ-2 closely matched those observed in the initial CCCZ-2, indicating the robust preservation of its core structural characteristics [67]. Thus, CCCZ-2 demonstrated substantial potential for practical applications due to its outstanding reusability and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of adsorptive removal of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR and XPS analyses were conducted to evaluate the characteristics of CCCZ-2 prior to and following Cr(VI) adsorption, aiming to elucidate the adsorption mechanism. In Fig. 6 a, a notable decrease in peak intensity is evident for the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations at around 3352 cm−1 upon Cr(VI) adsorption [68]. Additionally, the emergence of a new peak at 904 cm−1 provided evidence for the presence of Cr species [69]. These findings clearly established an association between Cr(VI) adsorption and hydroxyl and amino groups.

Fig. 6b compares the full-scan XPS spectra of CCCZ-2 before and after Cr(VI) adsorption. The appearance of a Cr2p peak verified the effective adsorption of Cr(VI) by CCCZ-2. In Fig. 6c, the peaks at 585.7 eV and 576.0 eV were assigned to the Cr2p1/2 and Cr2p3/2 orbitals [70], respectively. The spin–orbit coupling of Cr2p1/2 and Cr2p3/2 at binding energies of 585.0 eV and 575.4 eV confirmed the presence of Cr(III), whereas the prevailing peaks at 587.0 eV and 577.4 eV indicated Cr2p1/2 and Cr2p3/2 for Cr(VI). These results confirmed the chemical reduction from Cr(VI) to Cr(III). The O1s XPS spectrum of CCCZ-2 (Fig. 6d) displays three peaks corresponding to Zn–O (531.7 eV), CO (532.7 eV), and C–O (533.7 eV), respectively. After Cr(VI) adsorption, these peaks exhibited shifts, indicating electrostatic interaction between the carboxyl group and Cr(III). Fig. 6e presents the N1s spectra of CCCZ-2 before Cr(VI) adsorption, revealing three peaks assigned to –NH3 + (401.0 eV), –NH2 (399.4 eV), and N– (398.8 eV). After Cr(VI) adsorption, a notable enhancement in the intensity of the spectral peak for –NH3 + verified the beneficial impact of the amino group on Cr(VI) adsorption.

Based on these analyses, a Cr(VI) sorption mechanism scheme was depicted in Fig. S14. Initially, Cr(VI) ions amassed on the surface of CCCZ-2 through electrostatic attraction. Subsequent to that, Cr(VI) ions penetrated the interior of CCCZ-2 via the engineered 3D porous structure. Some of the Cr(VI) ions underwent reduction to form Cr(III) ions, simultaneous with the oxidation of hydroxyl groups to carbonyl groups. The resulting Cr(III) ions were partly adsorbed onto CCCZ-2 by virtue of electrostatic interactions originating from the carboxyl groups and Cr(III) ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis DRS was employed to explore the light absorption characteristics of three prepared samples: ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 (Fig. 7 a). CCZ-2 displayed reduced absorbance in comparison to ZIF-8, possibly attributed to the dilution effect of ZIF-8 within CCZ-2. Conversely, the incorporation of CD in CCCZ-2 led to increased absorbance relative to CCZ-2. The corresponding band gap energies (Eg) for three samples were calculated as follows: ZIF-8 (4.89 eV), CCZ-2 (4.38 eV), and CCCZ-2 (4.25 eV), respectively [71].

VB-XPS was employed to analyze the valence bands of ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2, as shown in Fig. 7b [72]. Valence band potentials (EVB) were measured as 2.01 eV, 1.45 eV, and 1.28 eV for ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2, respectively. Furthermore, conduction band potentials (ECB) were determined using the formula ECB = EVB – Eg. The calculated ECB values for ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 were determined as −2.88 eV, −2.93 eV, and −2.97 eV, respectively.

Photoluminescent (PL) properties of ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7d, CCCZ-2 exhibited the lowest PL intensity among the samples [73]. This result confirmed more efficient separation of electron-hole pairs within CCCZ-2 [74]. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 were obtained, and the results are depicted in Fig. 7c. Notably, electron-hole pairs in CCCZ-2 exhibited an extended lifetime of 3.85 ns, contrasting with CCZ-2′s lifetime of 3.44 ns [75].

Transient photocurrent responses of ZIF-8, CCZ-2, and CCCZ-2 were investigated under intermittent UV light irradiation, and the results are depicted in Fig. 7e. The findings revealed that CCCZ-2 exhibited markedly higher efficiency in charge carrier separation compared to ZIF-8 and CCZ-2 [76].

Nyquist plots from the EIS analysis (Fig. 7f) demonstrated CCCZ-2′s notably smaller arc radius compared to other tested samples, signifying improved transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers [77]. Therefore, based on the analyses and discussions above, it was affirmed that CCCZ-2 exhibited superior photoelectric performance.

Active species trapping experiments were carried out to discern the primary active species involved in the photocatalytic mechanism, and the findings are depicted in Fig. 8 a [78]. The presence of BQ and AgNO3 resulted in reduced removal efficiencies of CCCZ-2 at 40 min, suggesting that •O2 − and e− served as the primary active species [79]. The addition of ethanol enhanced the removal efficiency through h+ recombination. Furthermore, the corresponding kapp value was determined as 0.1685 (Ethanol), 0.1102 (IPA), 0.0341 (AgNO3), and 0.0097 min−1 (BQ), respectively (Fig. 8b). Additionally, DMPO-EPR spectroscopy was employed to validate the active species [80]. Following an 8-minute irradiation period (as illustrated in Fig. 8c), a distinct DMPO-•O2 − signal emerged. These results suggested that ZIF-8 within CCCZ-2 generated e− and •O2 −, enhancing the efficiency of Cr(VI) removal [81].

The improved photocatalytic performance can be attributed to the establishment of a Z-scheme charge transfer mode (Fig. S15), facilitating the efficient transfer and separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Upon UV light irradiation, both CN-CD and ZIF-8 could readily generate photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Photogenerated electrons originating from the CB of CN-CD rapidly recombined with photogenerated holes at the VB of ZIF-8, resembling an all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalytic system. This system comprised CN-CD, ZIF-8, and CD, with CD fulfilling the role of an electron mediator. Consequently, a greater number of photogenerated electrons from ZIF-8 participated in the reduction of Cr(VI) due to ZIF-8′s more negative CB potential (−2.88 eV) in contrast to E0 (Cr(VI)/Cr(III), 0.51 eV). Meanwhile, the preserved h+ within CN-CD's VB facilitated the oxidation of OH− to ⋅OH because CN-CD possessed a more positive VB potential (2.70 eV) than E0 (OH−/⋅OH, 2.40 eV).

Fig. 9 illustrates the synergistic mechanism of adsorption and photocatalysis in CCCZ, summarized in three main parts. Part I: CCCZ's 3D porous structure contains notable active sites, particularly the amino group of CD. The protonated amino group in CCCZ strongly attracts Cr(VI) ions electrostatically, facilitating their rapid adsorption onto the CCCZ surface. Part II: Under UV light exposure, CCCZ becomes excited and generates electrons. These generated electrons interact effectively with Cr(VI) ions, leading to the formation of Cr(III) ions. Additionally, certain photogenerated electrons react with O2, producing •O2 −, which enhances the reduction of Cr(VI) ions to Cr(III) ions. Part III: Repulsive electrostatic forces between the positively charged CD and Cr(III) ions induce the detachment of Cr(III) ions. This detachment process generates accessible active sites for subsequent adsorption processes. Hence, the primary reaction steps involved in Cr(VI) removal by CCCZ can be expressed through the following equations.

This study presented the synthesis of a novel photocatalytic adsorbent named CCCZ, derived from CS, TN, CD, and ZIF-8. CCCZ exhibited exceptional long-term stability and ease of separation. Notably, CCCZ displayed a maximum adsorption capacity of 500.08 mg/g, surpassing that of pure CS gel bead by 3.4 times. The apparent rate constant of CCCZ was determined as 0.1150 min−1, surpassing that of the original ZIF-8 by a factor of 5.7. This outstanding removal performance of CCCZ can be attributed to the 3D porous structure formed by CN-CD and ZIF-8, enhancing both adsorption and photocatalytic properties. These findings indicate that CCCZ holds significant potential as a stable, easily separable, and integrated photocatalytic adsorbent for efficient Cr(VI) removal.
